data.title: DoorDash sees double-digit growth in orders, GOV; sets upbeat Q1 guidance
data.description:
DoorDash (DASH) shares reversed a knee-jerk loss on Q4 results and have advanced more than 10% as a top- and bottom-line miss was overshadowed by a beat on the number of total orders, the cash value of orders, and upbeat guidance for the current quarter.   

Total orders increased 32% to 903M, while marketplace gross order value ((GOV)) increased 39% to $29.7M, both of which beat expectations of 885M and $29.17M, respectively.

However, although total sales rose 36.6% to $3.96B and profits on a per-share basis increased 45% to $.048, both missed expectations by $30M and $0.10, respectively, even as estimates for both were lowered ahead of the release.

Moreover, gross margin narrowed 150 basis points to 52.6%, and net revenue margin narrowed 40 basis points to 13.1%. Adjusted EBITDA of $780M was up 38% year-over-year and straddled the $710M-$810M estimated range, but adjusted EBITDA margin as a percentage of marketplace GOV narrowed 10 basis points to 2.6%.

After making heavy investments in the business last quarter (up 41% from 2024), DoorDash (DASH) said it anticipates “significant levels of ongoing investment in new categories and international markets, as well as growing investment in new initiatives and a global technology platform.”

And while the company historically exceeded the high end of the guidance range for Marketplace GOV, the current outlook for Q1 “reflects Marketplace GOV within our guided range.”

Accordingly, DoorDash (DASH) expects Q1 Marketplace GOV to be between $31B and $31.8B (versus $30.8B estimates) and adjusted EBITDA of $675M to $775M, both of which are significant gains from the same quarter last year.

data.title: Bausch Health declines on Q4 profit miss
data.description:
• Bausch Health Companies (BHC) is down ~4% in after-hours trading Wednesday after posting Q4 2025 financial results that missed on the bottom line.
  • Bausch posted GAAP EPS of ($0.30). The Street was expecting EPS of $0.42. In Q4 2024, Bausch had GAAP EPS of $0.25.
  • Revenue of ~$2.8B was a ~9.5% year-over-year increase and a beat. Consensus was $2.71B. 
  • The company also released 2026 revenue guidance of $10.625B-$10.875B. Consensus is $10.52B.
  • Bausch was hurt in Q4 by a ~16% YoY increase in expenses to ~$2.3B.
  • The company ended 2025 with cash, cash equivalents, and restricted cash of $1.325B compared to $1.2B on Dec. 31, 2024

data.title: ProPetro reaffirms 1 GW PROPWR target by 2030 while advancing disciplined capital strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ProPetro Holding Corp. (PUMP) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Sam Sledge highlighted that 2025 was marked by a significant slowdown in completions activity, with "the Permian... operating with approximately 70 full-time frac fleets, down meaningfully from 90 to 100 fleets just a year ago." Sledge emphasized that "ProPetro continued to deliver both operationally and financially and generated strong free cash flow, particularly in the fourth quarter." He reiterated the company's focus on disciplined cost control, stating, "we continue to control what we can and move quickly by streamlining costs across the business."
  • Sledge confirmed the majority of active frac fleets remain under contract, providing ongoing stability, and outlined strategic capital allocation plans: "we plan to continue to allocate capital to our FORCE electric equipment, given its strong demand and commercial leverage...prior to committing to additional FORCE equipment orders, we require greater visibility into customer demand and growth."
  • Sledge detailed ongoing investments to support growth, including "targeted capital to refurbish a portion of our existing Tier IV DGB fleet, make investments in fleet automation technology as well as measured investments in direct drive gas frac units."
  • The CEO reported significant progress in PROPWR, stating "total committed capacity to now approximately 240 megawatts and to also deploy our first assets into the field." He added, "With this order, PROPWR's equipment portfolio is split approximately 70% and 30% between high-efficiency natural gas reciprocating engine generators and low emission modular turbines, respectively."
  • Sledge reaffirmed, "we are positioned to deliver at least 750 megawatts by year-end 2028 and 1 gigawatt or more by year-end 2030."
  • CFO Caleb Weatherl stated, "ProPetro's performance in the fourth quarter and throughout 2025 showcased the results of our strategy at work. Through disciplined cost control efforts and continued industrialization of our operations, we delivered resilient margins, strong free cash flow from our completions business despite a challenging market environment."

Outlook

  • Sledge stated, "while we expect market challenges to persist into 2026, we continue to control what we can and move quickly by streamlining costs across the business."
  • CFO Weatherl guided for full-year 2026 capital expenditures to be between $390 million and $435 million, with "the completions business...expected to account for $140 million to $160 million, including $40 million to $50 million related to lease buyouts for a portion of the company's FORCE electric fleet portfolio."
  • PROPWR capital expenditures in 2026 are expected to be "approximately $250 million to $275 million."
  • Sledge reaffirmed the "5-year growth outlook for PROPWR," maintaining a target of "at least 750 megawatts by year-end 2028 and 1 gigawatt or more by year-end 2030."

Financial Results

  • Weatherl reported total revenue for the fourth quarter was $290 million, a decrease of 1% compared to the third quarter. Net income was $1 million or $0.01 income per diluted share compared to net loss of $2 million or $0.02 loss per diluted share for the third quarter.
  • Adjusted EBITDA totaled $51 million, representing 18% of revenue and increasing 45% compared to the third quarter.
  • Free cash flow for the completions business was $98 million, supported by strong EBITDA performance and reduced completion CapEx.
  • As of December 31, 2025, total cash was $91 million and borrowings under the ABL credit facility were $45 million, with total liquidity at $205 million. As of January 31, 2026, total cash increased to $236 million, primarily from an equity offering generating $163 million in net proceeds.

Q&A

  • Derek Podhaizer, Piper Sandler: Asked about PROPWR contract mix and potential for additional data center contracts in 2026. Sledge responded, "it's definitely a portfolio approach...we think of a larger share of our work over time is non-oil and gas...some of these non-oil and gas data center, industrial type projects can be much bigger and chunkier in nature."
  • Podhaizer also inquired about frac market attrition and fleet sufficiency. Sledge replied, "I think that would be a major stretch for the existing pressure pumping market...we're very, very well positioned to capitalize on that structural tightness when it does come."
  • Arun Jayaram, JPMorgan: Asked about CapEx funding mix. Weatherl indicated, "we like to use cash on the balance sheet...but...have several flexible and competitive debt facilities...we'll plan to use a mix of those."
  • Stephen Gengaro, Stifel: Questioned power demand competition between oil and gas and data centers, and cost of power for e-fracs. Sledge stated, "I don't think we have any concern around e-frac power right now...that market is not really growing that fast right now."
  • Edward Kim, Barclays: Asked about the cost of power equipment for data centers. Simmering replied, "the $1.1 million that we've talked about is for the modular equipment we bought today, definitely works at certain power nodes in both the oil and gas and data center market."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone focused on execution risks, capital allocation, and market tightness, with questions probing contract mix, CapEx structure, and equipment costs. The sentiment was neutral, with some slightly positive undertones as analysts sought clarity on growth opportunities and capital discipline.
  • Management maintained a confident and disciplined tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A. Sledge used phrases such as "we are confident in our ability to continue to march down that path," and Weatherl reiterated commitment to disciplined capital deployment.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remains confident and focused, while analysts continue to probe for potential challenges and confirmation of execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for PROPWR remains unchanged, reaffirming targets of 750 megawatts by year-end 2028 and 1 gigawatt or more by year-end 2030.
  • 2026 CapEx guidance increased, with more detailed breakdown and explicit mention of lease buyouts for the FORCE fleet.
  • PROPWR reported expanded contract wins and increased equipment on order from 360 megawatts last quarter to 550 megawatts currently, reflecting accelerated growth.
  • Analysts continued to focus on power contract mix, data center opportunities, CapEx funding, and equipment costs, while management highlighted cost control and capital discipline.
  • Management tone stayed confident and forward-looking, with no material changes in sentiment from the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Sledge noted "market challenges to persist into 2026" and significant slowdown in completions activity.
  • Management expressed caution in capital deployment, indicating that "prior to committing to additional FORCE equipment orders, we require greater visibility into customer demand and growth."
  • Sledge warned of "winter weather in late January" impacting first quarter profitability.
  • Analyst questions highlighted risks related to CapEx funding mix, potential for power equipment cost increases, and competition for power assets between oilfield and data center applications.

Final Takeaway

ProPetro management underscores the company's operational resilience and financial discipline amid ongoing industry headwinds, with continued strong free cash flow generation supporting investments in next-generation equipment and the PROPWR segment. The company reaffirms its ambitious growth outlook for PROPWR, targeting 1 gigawatt or more by 2030 and 750 megawatts by year-end 2028, while maintaining a balanced and deliberate approach to capital deployment and contract mix. Management expresses confidence in the company’s strategy, operational execution, and ability to capitalize on evolving opportunities in both the oilfield and data center power markets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Fiverr outlines 2026 revenue range of $380M–$420M as company pivots to high-value, AI-driven work
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Fiverr International Ltd. (FVRR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Micha Kaufman stated that 2025 was "an execution year, and we delivered," highlighting 10% revenue growth and adjusted EBITDA of $92 million with a 21% margin. He emphasized, "Products like Dynamic Matching and managed services are enabling us to expand into larger, more complex projects and drive sustainable wallet share growth."
  • Kaufman described a strategic transformation: "We have since developed and begun executing a comprehensive multiyear plan to transform Fiverr from a transaction-oriented marketplace into a trusted work platform, one that enables businesses, AI models and agents to collaborate with talent on complex, high-value outcomes."
  • He noted that spend per buyer increased 13% year-over-year and that "buyers spending over $10,000 annually grew 7% and GMV from projects over $1,000 increased 23%." Kaufman announced the shift to prioritize complex, high-value projects and AI-native work: "Today, projects over $1,000 represent less than 15% of marketplace GMV, yet they are growing 23% year-over-year. With focused execution, we believe this segment will become a materially larger contributor to our business."
  • Kaufman outlined a multiyear execution plan around four pillars: matching, product, go-to-market, and operational excellence, with "tangible impact within 4 to 6 quarters."
  • On leadership, Kaufman revealed that Ofer Katz will transition from CFO to focus on strategic investments and M&A, Esti will become CFO, and Jinjin will assume the newly created Chief Business Officer role.
  • President Ofer Katz noted, "There is a strong alignment, clarity and conviction on the direction we are taking. Most importantly, there is a shared sense of purpose that ties us back to Fiverr's founding 16 years ago."
  • Katz stated, "We delivered fourth quarter revenue of $107.2 million, up 3% year-over-year, while achieving record adjusted EBITDA and adjusted EBITDA margin. Adjusted EBITDA for Q4 was $26.5 million, representing an adjusted EBITDA margin of 25%."

Outlook

  • Katz provided guidance: "For the full year 2026, we expect revenue to be in the range of $380 million to $420 million, representing year-over-year growth of negative 12% to negative 3%. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be in the range of $60 million to $80 million, representing an adjusted EBITDA margin of 18% at the midpoint."
  • For Q1 2026, "revenue is expected to be between $100 million and $108 million, representing year-over-year growth of negative 7% to 1%. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be $19 million to $23 million, representing an adjusted EBITDA margin of 20% at the midpoint."
  • Katz explained the wider guidance reflects "elevated uncertainty as we execute our transformational plan focused on high-value work alongside evolving market conditions."

Financial Results

  • Fiverr reported Q4 2025 revenue of $107.2 million, up 3% year-over-year.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for Q4 was $26.5 million with a margin of 25%.
  • Free cash flow in Q4 was $21.8 million.
  • Marketplace revenue was $71.5 million, driven by 3.1 million active buyers and $342 in spend per buyer.
  • GMV from transactions over $1,000 grew 22.8% year-over-year in Q4.
  • Services revenue in Q4 was $35.6 million, up 18% year-over-year and accounted for 33% of total revenue.
  • Katz noted repayment of a $460 million convertible note during Q4.

Q&A

  • Ronald Josey, Citi: Asked about core investments and M&A priorities. Kaufman responded that investment focus is "to deprioritize low-end and low-value transactions and focus most of our investment in high-end, high skilled, larger scope projects... that's what 2026 is all about."
  • Eric Sheridan, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about deprioritization impact. Kaufman stated, "We're shifting those resources into ensuring that the high-end portion of our business... will become a larger portion of our overall GMV contribution."
  • Bernard McTernan, Needham: Queried about margin profile and Fiverr Go. Katz said EBITDA margin would see "some lower margin... in the short term," but expects a return to 25% long-term EBITDA margin. Kaufman said Fiverr Go features are being integrated broadly rather than as a standalone product.
  • Charles Larkin (Chad), Oppenheimer: Asked about restructuring impact. Kaufman replied, "The revenue is not impacted by restructuring. It reflects the ongoing trends on the marketplace, meaning lower end versus higher end."
  • Marvin Fong, BTIG: Probed on timing for high-value work dominance and enterprise channel specifics. Kaufman said, "We haven't guided specifically for that, and we're not talking about percentage," but expects to see signals of segment growth in coming quarters.
  • Nathaniel Schindler, Scotiabank: Questioned product discontinuation and revenue trajectory. Kaufman assured, "We're not killing any of our products... we're just making sure that after the restructure, we have the vast majority of our resources to invest in the higher end."
  • Joshua Chan, UBS: Sought clarification on revenue outlook and free cash flow. Katz cited "combination of the trends we are seeing in Q4, together with the transformation" as creating uncertainty.
  • Matthew Condon, Citizens: Asked about recurring work and buybacks. Kaufman noted focus on "upgrading data infrastructure, matching algo product" for recurring work and reaffirmed "disciplined and balanced capital allocation."
  • Audrey Stuart, RBC: Asked about new business formation and SMB share. Kaufman said, "Business formation only impacts a small part of our catalog."
  • Rohit Kulkarni, ROTH Capital: Explored supply for high-value work and service revenue mix. Kaufman stated, "It's really very much around the data infrastructure, the matching algorithm that prioritizes quality and trust."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed skepticism and concern about revenue declines, recurring questions on the pace of transformation, and the durability of the business shift. The overall tone was slightly negative, with repeated requests for clarity on guidance and margin outlook.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing "conviction on the direction we are taking" and stating, "we expect tangible impact within 4 to 6 quarters." In Q&A, management used phrases like "let me be clear" and "we're very committed to execute this transformation with very high degree of financial discipline," indicating assertiveness but also acknowledgment of uncertainty.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts showed more urgency and caution, with management using more direct language about transformation and structural change, while analysts pressed harder on guidance and revenue risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from projecting 9% to 11% revenue growth for 2025 to forecasting a decline of 12% to 3% for 2026.
  • Management's tone evolved from emphasizing steady upmarket expansion and AI enablement to focusing on transformation, restructuring, and short-term revenue headwinds.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on the impact of macro trends and the pace of AI-driven transformation, but the latest call saw intensified concern over near-term revenue and margin compression.
  • Strategic focus shifted from steady product innovation and AI integration to an accelerated pivot toward high-value, complex, and AI-native work, deprioritizing lower-end segments.
  • Management stated that foundational investments and restructuring would "impact the adjusted EBITDA by approximately 200 basis points in 2026," while reiterating confidence in long-term profitability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted "elevated volatility in marketplace revenue this year compared to last year" as intentional deprioritization of low-value transactions impacts short-term performance.
  • Analysts repeatedly flagged uncertainty around the pace of revenue recovery and the timing of high-value work becoming the dominant contributor.
  • Management cited FX headwinds, particularly "appreciation of Israeli shekel to U.S. dollar," noting a $10 million headwind on EBITDA guidance.
  • Ongoing decline in low-skill, simple service categories, especially in programming, writing, and translation, driven by AI automation.
  • The company is not discontinuing any products but is "deprioritizing... optimization of these products in favor of developing the types of product experiences and the underlying technologies and infrastructure to address the higher-end project."

Final Takeaway

Fiverr’s leadership emphasized that 2026 will be a transformational year as the company accelerates its shift toward high-value, complex, and AI-native work, deprioritizing lower-value transactions. Leadership changes and foundational investments are intended to position Fiverr for a return to growth in 2027 and beyond, with guidance reflecting short-term revenue contraction and margin pressure as these shifts take hold. Management remains confident in the long-term profitability of the core business and its ability to leverage its talent network and proprietary data for sustainable growth in the evolving work landscape.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Acadian Timber appoints Malcolm Cockwell as interim CEO
data.description:
• Acadian Timber (ADN:CA) on Wednesday announced Malcolm Cockwell has been appointed interim President & Chief Executive Officer, effective immediately.
  • Cockwell succeeds Adam Sheparski, who has stepped down as President & CEO and as a Director of the company to pursue other opportunities.
  • Cockwell has served as Chair of the company since August 2019. 
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Molson Coors Non-GAAP EPS of $1.21 beats by $0.06, revenue of $2.66B misses by $50M
data.description:
• Molson Coors press release (TAP): Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of $1.21 beats by $0.06.
  • Revenue of $2.66B (-2.9% Y/Y) misses by $50M.

  • 2026 OUTLOOK

    We expect to achieve the following targets for full year 2026 despite the inherent uncertainties that exist with inflationary commodity cost pressures and a softer beer industry.

    • Net sales: flat, plus or minus 1% versus 2025 on a constant currency basis.
    • Underlying income (loss) before income taxes: decline in the range of 15% to 18% versus 2025 on a constant currency basis.
    • Underlying earnings per share: decline in the range of 11% to 15% versus 2025.
    • Capital expenditures: $650 million incurred, plus or minus 5%.
    • Underlying free cash flow: $1.1 billion, plus or minus 10%.
    • Underlying depreciation and amortization: $720 million, plus or minus 5%.
    • Consolidated net interest expense: $260 million, plus or minus 5%.
    • Underlying effective tax rate: in the range of 22% to 24% for 2026.
  • Shares -7.81% AH.

data.title: Blue Owl Capital Corp. Q4 earnings meet consensus; TII trails
data.description:
Blue Owl Capital Corp. (OBDC) delivered Q4 earnings on Wednesday that met the Wall Street consensus as its adjusted total investment income fell short of expectations.

The business development company's stock, though, surged 5.7% in after-hours trading as OBDC and two sister BDCs announced agreements to sell $1.4B of assets, giving OBDC greater flexibility to deploy capital.

Q4 adjusted net investment income per share of $0.36, matching the average analyst estimate, compared with $0.36 in Q3 and $0.47 in Q4 2024.

Q4 adjusted total investment income of $439.5M, vs. the $450.9M consensus, declined from $446.3M in the prior quarter and increased from $394.4M in the year-ago period.

Net asset value per share of $14.81 at Dec. 31, 2025, dipped from $14.89 at Sept. 30, driven primarily by credit-related markdowns on a small number of names, partly offset by accretive share repurchases.

Also on Wednesday, Blue Owl's (OBDC) approved a new repurchase program of up to $300M of common stock, replacing its prior $200M authorization approved in November 2025.

"OBDC closed the year with strong fourth-quarter earnings and credit performance, reflecting the health of our borrowers and our defensive, senior secured strategy focused on the upper middle market," said CEO Craig W. Packer. "Demonstrating our conviction in OBDC's strategy and long-term value, we repurchased approximately $148M of OBDC's common stock during the quarter, accretive to NAV per share and the largest quarterly repurchase activity in our history."

Total investments at fair value fell to $16.5B at Dec. 31, 2025, from $17.1B at Sept. 30.

Net investment commitments during the quarter totaled $684M, and sales and repayments were $1.3B. That compared with $1.3B in new investment commitments and $797M of sales and repayments in Q3. 

Conference call on Feb. 19 at 10:00 AM ET.

data.title: Insulet outlines 21%–23% Omnipod revenue growth for 2026 with operating margin expansion, driven by global adoption and product innovation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Insulet Corporation (PODD) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Ashley McEvoy, CEO, reported "we closed 2025 with another strong quarter, recording our 10th consecutive year of 20% or greater constant currency revenue growth," citing durable recurring revenue and a profitable business model as key drivers. She highlighted total company revenues of $784 million for the quarter, 29% growth in constant currency, and surpassing $2.7 billion in full year revenue.
  • McEvoy emphasized the company's leadership in automated insulin delivery (AID), particularly with Omnipod 5, and noted record new customer starts across both U.S. and international markets, with the majority transitioning from multiple daily injections. She stated, "Omnipod's strong clinical evidence, broad access, affordability and ease of use are enabling us to expand well beyond traditional endocrinology channels."
  • CEO McEvoy discussed momentum in the U.S. type 2 diabetes market, with type 2 new customer starts growing significantly both sequentially and year-over-year, and the prescriber base expanding 62% to over 6,500 clinicians in 2025. She described international performance as "standout," supported by launches in Canada, Australia, and the Middle East, along with positive price/mix realization from the transition to Omnipod 5.
  • McEvoy announced that in 2026, Insulet will introduce new algorithm updates, expand CGM integrations, and launch Omnipod Discover, a data platform to enhance prescriber and patient experience. She added, "In 2026, we will continue to purposely increase R&D investment to advance our next-generation platforms, including Omnipod 6 as well as our fully closed-loop system for type 2 diabetes."
  • Flavia Pease, CFO, stated, "delivering over $780 million in total revenue, an increase of 31.2% at reported rates and 29% at constant currency rates." She highlighted record new customer starts, stable retention rates, and expansion of gross margin to 72.5% for the quarter and 71.6% for the year. Pease also noted a 50% increase in R&D spending in Q4, and 37% for the full year.

Outlook

  • Pease outlined 2026 guidance, stating, "we expect our total Omnipod revenue to grow 21% to 23% and our total company revenue to grow 20% to 22%."
  • U.S. Omnipod revenue is expected to grow 20% to 22% in 2026, with international Omnipod revenue projected to grow 24% to 26%. Pease attributed the deceleration in growth to difficult comparisons due to the anniversary of the U.S. type 2 launch and annualizing recent international rollouts.
  • The company expects approximately 100 basis points of operating margin expansion for the full year, reflecting top line growth, modest gross margin improvement, and a step-up in R&D investments.
  • Pease also shared, "we expect 2026 adjusted EPS to increase by more than 25%."

Financial Results

  • Total revenue for Q4 2025 was reported as $784 million, with U.S. revenues of $568 million and international revenues of $214 million.
  • Full year 2025 revenue surpassed $2.7 billion, with $1.9 billion from the U.S. and $754 million from international markets.
  • Pease reported a fourth quarter gross margin of 72.5% and full year gross margin of 71.6%. Adjusted operating margin was 18.7% for Q4 and 17.6% for the year. Adjusted EPS was $1.55 for Q4 and $4.97 for the year.
  • Free cash flow for 2025 exceeded $375 million, including a $70 million tax benefit. Capital expenditures for Q4 were $135 million, mainly attributed to manufacturing expansion in Malaysia and Costa Rica.

Q&A

  • Jeffrey Johnson, Baird: What is the most underappreciated part of the Insulet story? McEvoy: "I would highlight 4 key areas. Number one is our tech lead...number two is our growing commercial prowess...three is our manufacturing at scale; and four is our financial strength."
  • Robert Marcus, JPMorgan: How do you sustain new patient growth in the U.S. and internationally? McEvoy responded that balanced growth is driven primarily by new entrants from MDI, with type 2 at nascent stages and a strong clinical value proposition.
  • David Roman, Goldman Sachs: Can you help reconcile script trends to reported revenue? Pease said, "the best...would be to use total Pods as that's the best reflection of the future revenue outlook," noting script data has limitations.
  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: How sustainable is 20% growth amid new competition? McEvoy emphasized Insulet's head start, barriers to entry, and ongoing investment in clinical performance and commercial efforts. Pease added, "We have best-in-class gross margin...We are free cash flow positive."
  • Michael Polark, Wolfe Research: Is Insulet ready for Dexcom's 15-day G7 sensor? Benjamin stated, "Omnipod 5 is compatible with the 15-day G7 now."
  • Travis Steed, BofA: How has guidance philosophy changed? Pease: "We continue to set guidance with a full intent to deliver. That has not changed."
  • Joanne Wuensch, Citi: What should we look for at ADA? McEvoy and Benjamin highlighted upcoming data on the fully closed-loop system for type 2 and pivotal results from the STRIVE study for Omnipod 6.
  • Richard Newitter, Truist: Does 40% of new starts from type 2 signal type 1 growth leveling off? Pease said type 1 grew year-over-year and was comparable to Q3; type 2 growth is accelerating due to lower penetration.
  • Danielle Antalffy, UBS: How does the in-practice sampling program work? McEvoy described a capital-efficient approach with strong conversion ratios and expanding prescriber base.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions on sustainability of growth, competition, and new patient acquisition, indicating a neutral-to-slightly-positive tone with focus on long-term differentiation and execution.
  • Management maintained a confident tone throughout, emphasizing execution, innovation, and competitive moats. McEvoy repeatedly pointed to the company's "track record," "tech lead," and "financial strength."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management continue to focus on market expansion and competition, though management's confidence appeared more pronounced in this call, especially regarding barriers to entry and innovation pipeline.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from raising 2025 forecasts last quarter to explaining decelerating growth rates for 2026 due to tough comps and annualizing major launches.
  • Strategic focus remains on expanding type 2 penetration and international markets, with new emphasis on next-generation platforms (Omnipod 6, closed-loop system).
  • Analysts this quarter focused more on sustainability, competitive threats, and growth drivers, while management placed greater emphasis on technology leadership, scale, and financial performance.
  • Key metric changes include a step-up in R&D spending, continued margin expansion, and a projected increase in EPS for 2026.
  • Management's tone shifted from strong optimism to highlighting durable moats and explaining growth normalization amid high comps.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited challenging year-over-year comparisons as a factor in decelerating growth guidance for 2026.
  • Analysts questioned sustainability of growth, competitive threats from new entrants, and the balance of type 1 versus type 2 growth.
  • Pease pointed to ongoing investments in innovation, commercial scale, and manufacturing as mitigation strategies, stating the company remains "well positioned to continue investing robustly for future growth while delivering meaningful margin expansion."

Final Takeaway

Insulet’s management highlighted another year of robust growth and successful global expansion, underpinned by a strong recurring revenue model, expanding market share in both type 1 and type 2 diabetes, and continued innovation through Omnipod 5 and next-generation platforms. For 2026, the company projects 21% to 23% Omnipod revenue growth and increased operating margins, supported by investments in R&D, manufacturing, and commercial capabilities. Management remains focused on sustaining leadership in AID, driving further penetration in underpenetrated markets, and delivering long-term value for shareholders through consistent execution and disciplined investment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
